speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I'm a silver [INAUDIBLE] master, which means I have 1,500 points. And I've won some national tournaments. And I enjoy playing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times a week do you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five times.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could play Seven times, but I don't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to have to be careful about that ring. Do you hear it, [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK? OK. Talk a little more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that I shouldn't make a noise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand. Oh, OK. What else can I tell you? I miss my husband very badly now. And I dreamt during last night that he came back right from the grave. He came from the gave, sat on my bed and was talking to me. Now, I said, how could manage-- manage that. And I [INAUDIBLE] real good. And he said, well, I managed it, because I wanted to. We had a nice conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is September 1, 1995. The survivor is Magda Vass. Her maiden name was Magda Vago. The interviewer is Cathy Epstien. We're in Deerfield Beach, Florida United States of America. The language is English. This is tape number one. Today's date is September 1, 1995. My name is Cathy Epstein, C-A-T-H-Y E-P-S-T-E-I-N. I'm interviewing Magda Vass. M-A-G-D-A V-A-S-S, who's a survivor of the Nazi Holocaust. We're in Deerfield Beach, Florida, in the United States of America. And the language is English. Could you please tell me your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda Vass.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda Vago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now is that your original family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father's origin name was Weisz, W-E-I-S-Z in the Hungarian spelling. But before this-- his second child was born, he changed it to Hungarian name. Because he thought it was good for business, too, to have a Hungarian sounding name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born February 9, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which makes you how old today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kaposvar, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-P-O-S-V-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what part of Hungary was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was best part of Hungary, about an hour drive-- train ride from Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish population of Kaposvar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you exactly. It was a good-sized Jewish population. We had a very nice, large synagogue, which was kind of conservative. And then we had a smaller orthodox synagogue. I couldn't tell you, but it was a good-sized Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family conservative, or Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Conservative.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Louis Vago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vah-go. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a bakery. My parents had a bakery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Josephine. Maiden name was [INAUDIBLE], which was a very rare name. It really meant owning land in German. And-- his parents or grandparents, I don't know which, they owned some land which was a very rare thing for Jews in Hungary to own land.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you mother work also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother just helped in the store to wait on people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about brothers and sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked-- she worked plenty, because she had six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your brothers' and sisters' names then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name was Rose. My older brother's name was Dezso, D-E-Z-S-O. Then came Andor, like Andrew, A-N-D-O-R, then Gorge, G-O-R-G-E, and Imre, I-M-R-E. And then I was born, eight years later, later than my youngest brother, eight years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the baby of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the baby, and I had too many supervisors. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life growing up in Kaposvar.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very happy childhood. I had my four brothers, who were absolutely like angels to me, and my sister, who was like a second mother. And I just-- I went to a private school. I had a very good education. i had-- I studied five languages-- four, and the Hungarian, of course, German, Italian, and France, and Latin. I had five years of Latin, five years of Italian. And German, I had about 12 years or more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I enjoyed school. I was an excellent student. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can tell you I got the married. My first husband's name was Frank Rosenberg. But he died during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your memories of holidays as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd say that we had a lot of people. We had a huge table. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Seder went kind of fast, because my father wasn't a very religious man. And he-- he tried to page the Haggadah two, three pages at a time. Because he was anxious to get the soup and everything else. My mother was an excellent cook. He was-- she was a gourmet cook. And high holidays, we all went to the synagogue. I went to a Jewish elementary school. And, otherwise, we were not very observant Jews, went for the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember any incidences of anti-Semitism growing up in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I really don't. I had very nice Christian friends who came to our house. And I went to theirs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It started only when Hitler came to power. In you know, Hungary-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Hungary, we heard all the news, the radio, the newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew what was going on, but not completely. Because, first of all, we didn't really believe it. And even at that time, when it started, I didn't hear anything-- So there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --personally,

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a lot of interaction between the Jews and gentiles in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. No, I wouldn't say a lot. But there was some.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your first memories of things changing in Kaposvar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's see. I was a secretary to a bank president. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when it started there, that they fired Jews. They just lost their jobs. Now, my-- one of my brothers was the director of a branch office of one of the largest banks in Hungary. Director means he was the manager. He was in charge of everything. It was a very excellent position. And he was sent to a little village. So he lost that job. He-- he was sent to be the director in a bank for a little village. And that was a terrible shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the last person in my hometown to be fired. Because, first of all, it was like I did a good job. Second, which was interesting, the director at that time was a Jewish man. And he was fired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a new director coming in whose brother was the chief Nazi in Hungary. He was in charge of everything. It was called Arrow Cross-- [INAUDIBLE] the German name-- which was, what-- what kind of cross? I don't remember now the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, his wife was Jewish. And he was very nice to me. He knew what's coming. Actually, he escaped with his wife and was hiding all through the war to safely survive. And they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meshco.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when finally I was fired? 1940, 1941. And then I got married anyhow. But I don't exactly-- but then it started to be that they put pressure on people not to employ Jews. And Jews in positions were fired.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other restrictions were put on Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, I really couldn't tell you. It later came when we were put in the ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was, I think, in early 1943. And that was 1943, early spring. And as far as I know, our town was the last one in Hungary that-- that Jews were put into a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a mayor of the city who happened to have a Jewish lover. He was married, but he had a Jewish woman lover. We all knew that. Everybody knew it. And she-- he held it off as long as he could. Actually, the ghetto started where that woman lived with her husband and the child. He had a-- she had a sickly husband. And then it went on to the neighborhood where the temple was. And that was the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the mayor's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His original name, I don't. But he changed his name to Kaposvari, the name of the city, plus there was a I or a Y added to the very end. Like if would say, Deerfield Beachi. [LAUGHS] He changed his name to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his mistress' name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His mistress' name was Mrs. Furst, F-U-R-S-T. His first name, I don't remember. Had a daughter, Klara, who was a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before Kaposvar was made into a ghetto, what was your life like in those couple of years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right before wasn't good anymore, because my husband was taken to a labor camp. All of my brothers, my sister's husband, everybody-- the men under-- I don't know-- 50 maybe, or 55, they were all taken to labor camps. Once in a while, they came back for a furlough. But they had to go. It was just like if you're called in the army, you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the same thing. Because many people pose very stupid questions saying, well, why didn't you resist? Why did you went to the ghetto? Why did you go to Auschwitz? Why didn't you resist? That is such a disturbing, stupid question. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were there, women, children, and men, really old. What could we do when they came with guns? There was no way for us to-- try to withstand and to say no, I'm not going. What stupid questions. We couldn't resist. They just hope for the best. And we were told-- we were-- we were already told that most likely there would be a transportation. Where? We didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we will up working. We heard that long ago. So I took up sewing, as a matter of fact. Because I was-- we were told that if you have a trade, nothing will happen to you, because you'll be put-- put to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't have any trade. I was very well-versed in the history of art and literature. And I knew it wouldn't do me any good. So I took up sewing. Now, when-- do you remember the day when they told you that the ghetto was being formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Spring of '93-- 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day that you left your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband was already-- he was [INAUDIBLE] in a labor camp. So I moved into my parent's house, which was in the ghetto. So I moved in there. And there are a lot of people who lived there, because my parents had a big home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of these-- all Jews and some relatives, an aunt of mine and a cousin, and my sister, and my niece-- and there are a lot of other people. Friends, neighbors, almost  neighbors who were out of the ghetto, they were put into our home. We had a five bedroom home, my parents did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there we were. We had no trouble as far as food was considered. Because first of all, my parents had a big yard. And behind the yard, we had a big lot which was like a vegetable garden. That was the custom in Hungary when you have that-- that you hired people to work there. And like, my parents gave the land and all the expenses for food or fertilizer-- I mean, for seed and fertilizer. And then, it was half and half. Whatever were the crop, the people who worked got half, and we got half. So we had no trouble. But we had trouble at that time taking care, because they couldn't come in the ghetto. They were gentile people And so we worked. I worked. I weeded. I worked with the potatoes. I remember that clearly. Next door was also ghetto and some people were put in. And there was a man who-- whose daughter I was tutoring in math. I was very good in math.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he has seen me-- what's that-- hoeing the potato plants. And he says to me through the fence-- we had a fence separating the two yards. He says, Magda, that's why the Jews will survive everything. They can't kill us. I said, what are you talking about? We're [? going to get all killed. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Look, you are working in the yard, hoeing potatoes. Because I was respected very much as a student and so forth. That's the proof that we will all survive. [LAUGHS] I don't know what made him say that, but it kind of stuck to my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a closed ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a barbed wire, yes. We could go out a certain hour in the morning to a store to buy bread or whatever like that if needed. But it was a hassle, because everyone-- we had just so much time. I don't remember. Half an hour, an hour. I couldn't tell you. But it was a hassle to go and get the bread, or eggs, or whatever, which didn't grow in the yard, in the vegetative garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a hassle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other restrictions did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't go out. That was it. And nobody could come in. And one day, my favorite teacher-- which is in the book too, because my niece was his favorite. And I was his favorite. He wasn't Jewish. He came to the fence. And he looked at us. And he was crying. I could see his eyes, his tears on his face. And he waved to me, and I waved back. And he even gave a book through the fence to my niece. And then he went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's bakery in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because that house-- that was a big house, a big yard. And at the end of the yard was the bakery. In the yard, close to the house was a beautiful flowerbed. My mother was always very attached to flowers. As matter of fact, in one of the pictures you can even see a little trace of geraniums behind the window. But he had a flower bed. She had a flower bed, a bigger one and some little ones. In that flower bed, the night before we were transported, we tried to same some jewelry, which we all had to give in. But this good bracelet I had, was given to me back my youngest brother when I was 16 years old. And we-- I was hiding that. My niece and I we worked all night long hiding things and hiding things in the cellar, like good fat, and papers, and pictures, my-- my-- my matriculation paper which I have, and whatever, some jewelry, some of my mother's silver, flatware, sterling silver, which has the initials engraved, VJV. And I have some spoons from that I saved and two big ladles, one big, one small. One was [NON-ENGLISH] one was [NON-ENGLISH]. That I have. But, otherwise, we gave in everything. We were told if you don't, you may be transported. [LAUGHS] So we did. Radio we had to give into the city. And because we all had maids-- which it wasn't a big thing in Hungary. Because it was-- maids came in from the villages, where they were very poor. And they had their shelter. And they had all the food and a little money, which they saved. And they went back to the villages and got married, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very cheap. Everybody had a maid. It didn't show any big richness to have a maid. So these things that you hid in the garden--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we had-- we-- we hid in the flower bed. It was really not the-- a garden. It was a flower bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you able to retrieve them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back after the war in 1945, Fall in October. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by that, my niece was engaged. And her husband had a very big position in the American Army. He has several medals and letters from Eisenhower. He was a hero. And he took me to Prague. And in Prague, he put me on a train. I went back to Hungary. And there, I heard that everybody died in my family. And, of course, I went to my parent's home. And I thought they wouldn't let me in. The people who lived there. They wouldn't let me in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But [INAUDIBLE], I went over to a neighbor who also survived and got some kind of shovel and started to dig. And they called the gendarmes. You know what the gendarmes are? They're worse than the police. They called the gendarmes trying to dig for treasures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they felt everything belonged to them, not to me. So I was desperate. And I had-- my best girlfriend's husband was an attorney. And they both survived. And I went over there. And he was home, and he came. And, also, I went to the second house where I borrowed that shovel from where a former boyfriend of mine lived who survive, and who wanted to marry me. But I didn't marry him. And he came. And so there was a big discussion and so forth. And then finally, they agreed that I can have it. So that's how I have those things which I have, but that was a really very frightening experience. I was afraid they would put me in jail or something. I went back to my home where I lived with my husband. It was a two-story building. Downstairs was my mother-in-law who was living. Of course, she died in Auschwitz, too. And upstairs, my husband and I were living. And they wouldn't let me in there either. I looked to the door. I could see my kitchen furniture. They wouldn't let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back to the time in the ghetto when you found out that the ghetto was being liquidated. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. We-- in the ghetto, we had something like a governing body. Maybe that's the wrong expression. But they were in charge to take care of get everything to go right in the ghetto, you know, nobody should escape. Oh, [INAUDIBLE]. I have to go back to that. My youngest brother did one night. And-- they were in communication with the SS, or the Hungarian Arrow Cross. That was the Hungarian SS. And they were told that we have to get ready, that the following day, we will be transported. We had to go to the temple. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Close to the temple was the Jewish elementary school. That was a-- I don't remember-- two, three, or four story, big building. And that's where we had to go. So we all got together-- and we were told what you can have. Just a little package.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you bring with in that package?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started to hide a few things-- sewing in shoulder pads in our coats. It was in summer, but we knew we have to be prepared for winter. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] toothpaste, we put in gold chain, in toothpaste, or in gold pin and some Hungarian money, which we didn't know if it was good for anything. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's what they did, and underwear and some shoes, something lightweight so we could carry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I remember vaguely that there was like a wagon we pulled somethings to the-- to that school. But I really don't remember exactly. Maybe because my mother was old, we put in her things in that wagon, we pulled to that school. And, of course, there, we went in. And we were examined, which was the most humiliating experience. Very rude women reached into our vagina to see if we hide anything there. If you can imagine, my niece was 18 years old-- 18 or-- '44-- 19 years old, reaching up into her vagina. And everywhere, under arm, on our head, everywhere, they examined us.
